response: The internet originated from 1960s U.S. Department of Defense-funded computer networking research, which produced ARPANET—first demonstrated in 1969 via a UCLA-to-Stanford Research Institute message; 1983’s adoption of TCP/IP protocols laid the modern internet’s technical foundation, while Tim Berners-Lee’s 1989 CERN-developed World Wide Web (with HTML, URLs, and HTTP) and 1993’s Mosaic browser opened the web to non-expert users. Subsequent inflection points included the late 1990s dot-com boom (and 2001 crash that elevated survivors like Amazon and Google into global corporate giants), mid-2000s social media platforms (Facebook, Twitter, YouTube), 2006’s launch of AWS (pioneering cloud computing), and 2007’s iPhone, which fueled the mobile internet revolution. Today, the internet connects over 5 billion people, serving as the indispensable backbone for nearly every facet of modern life, from commerce and communication to entertainment.
